data.title: Victory Capital: Screaming Hold Wherever You Look
data.description:
Victory Capital (NASDAQ:VCTR), a diversified multi-boutique, global asset management firm, appears to be doing well financially and is definitely a fundamentally sound investment. I see it as a cash generating business of long term strategic value. However, wherever I see, from balance sheets to share prices, I see stagnation. Also, short-term catalysts are worrying.

Macro will Hurt Fees and AUM

Expect markets to be volatile and upside capped unless something changes drastically on the trade war and economic stagnation front. The short-term damage, set in already, is sufficient to drag net inflows into asset management firms across the board. Additionally, fee compression threats will come into play.

AUM impacts one multiplier of the revenue and there VCTR has been a story of net outflows for a few quarters now. After a brief acute redemption pressure in 4Q 2022, the AUM continued to recover (though still net outflow). Since Q2 2024, net outflow has increased again. The market volatility in Q1 2025 will not be too kind on this metric and that is likely to continue for a few quarters.

VCTR has also already implemented expense waivers and reimbursements for certain funds. Zero commission fees on trading is also likely to be a continuing pressure if trader interest wanes - in a market already pressed for lower trading costs amid widespread competition. Fee structures are the other important multiplier in the revenue outcome.

While overall revenue has not suffered significantly in the past few quarters, it has had blips and stagnation in the past 4 years as a result of these two strong segment trends - something that can only get worse before getting any better.

A Look at Developments and Numbers

VCTR has been trying to reignite the growth spark through partnerships, acquisitions, and platform improvements. The last game-changing acquisition, a rerating trigger, was the acquisition of USAA Asset Management Company in 2019, which gave VCTR access to the US military retirement scheme. This changed the VCTR brand perception, in addition to a foothold in a sticky client base. Nothing visible in terms of strategy or industry trends supports such an explosion as of now.

Partnership with Amundi, expected to close by Q1 2025, will drive cost synergies. The ETF platform and digital reach has been significant over many years now. Interest Expenses, which showed signs of decrease in Q4 2024, will likely continue to benefit if the interest rate regime remains docile or further rate cuts happen.

Overall expenses too have remained within range - no negative surprises there. However, as Revenues have stagnated, a rangebound cost control environment has meant rangebound gross and operating margins. Overall, the story has been one of strong financial numbers, nothing to complain about, but screams stagnation whichever way you look at it. While strategic acquisitions and partnerships have helped drive cost synergies, platform improvements and improved offerings, VCTR looks like waiting for an explosion moment on top line as well as bottom line and the current situation definitely does not place it well for that.

Valuation Neither Here Nor There

With a forward PE of 9.22, VCTR is valued higher than its 5-year average, which is surprising, given the headline stagnation evident everywhere in its business. Valuation is reasonable compared to the sector median, but it's not a buy magnet on valuation alone. EV/EBITDA looks a lot cheaper, though, both in comparison to its own historical average as well as peers, but is fair given VCTR’s limited pricing power.

Price action also does not show any attractive corrections to demand a buy at this point. It appears like most of the financial soundness and existing business’ positives are well priced in.

The Seeking Alpha Quant Rating supports the bland view as well, with a Hold rating on the basis of growth and profitability on one hand, but neutral valuation and momentum indicators on the other.

Risks to the Thesis

While a lot of assumptions are based on declining inflows as the market corrects, we know how the last correction panned out for asset managers in 2020. Volatility may trigger more rebalancing, more investments on corrections, movement of funds between active and passive strategies depending on market structure. Volatile or bear markets are sometimes the best times that force product innovations, and maybe that is the trigger that catapults VCTR to the move we are waiting for. Of course, current market conditions are way different from the demand induced short-term market impacts in 2020. I believe this time, VCTR will give better entry point opportunity to fresh investors, even if it rebounds stronger.

Hold Victory Capital

The thesis is consistently a "Hold" call on Victory Capital from all counts. Existing investors will likely note the stable free cash flow, unlikely to dent chances of downgrades in dividend payouts, even if the business gets impacted in the next few quarters by global headwinds. While existing investors will find the existing yield of 3.37% (likely to continue) a cushion after having ridden the growth wave of the stock price appreciation, fresh investors may not find the yield compensating enough for the lack of growth prospects going ahead (and even an imminent threat of business drops). Existing investors can also consider booking partial profits depending on when they entered the VCTR journey.

data.title: MORT: A Strategic Buy For A Swing Trade (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

We last covered the VanEck Mortgage REIT Income ETF (NYSEARCA:MORT) last year, when we warned investors this fund was heavily reliant on rates, and would sell-off if rates moved higher:

As outlined above, we are of the opinion that the bulk of the move in rates is now behind us, with the market too aggressive, in our view. Currently, economic indicators are still strong, with the potential of a re-emergence in inflation.

Inflation has proven to be sticky indeed, and the market was pricing too many Fed cuts last year. MORT is down -14% on a total return basis since that article.

After the tariff induced market sell-off in the past few week, rates are back at their highs and the underlying components for MORT are heavily oversold. In today's piece we are going to outline a 'Buy' swing trade for the fund.

What is a swing trade?

As we have stated before, MORT is not a buy and hold, but more of a cyclical instrument with a very high beta. The name therefore tends to have very deep sell-offs but also offers significant upside after such moves. Let us first look at the definition of a 'swing trade':

A swing trade is a trading strategy where an investor buys and holds a financial asset, like a stock or forex, for a short to medium period—typically a few days to a few weeks—to profit from price fluctuations or "swings" in the market. The goal is to capture gains from an asset's price movement, either upward or downward, by identifying trends or patterns using technical analysis. Unlike day trading, swing trading doesn't require constant monitoring, but it involves higher risk than long-term investing due to market volatility over the holding period.

Swing trades fit MORT's profile perfectly. An investor therefore does not need to take a very long term view on each risk component for the fund, but follow signals driven by technical analysis. In the next sections, we are going to show you how the fund and its components are deeply oversold currently.

What does MORT contain? Various mReits and CRE Reits.

The fund contains a mix of mReits and CRE Reits:

Please note a high concentration in Annaly (NLY), AGNC (AGNC) and Starwood (STWD). Each of these names is driven mainly by rates and risk-on / risk-off, but they have significant idiosyncrasies. In today's piece, we are, however, going to focus on their technicals, and propensity to become oversold in risk-off markets.

Vicious sell-off in MORT in the past month

As rates moved higher, MORT experienced a vicious sell-off in the past month:

Please note the high beta for the fund when compared to the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond (TLT) and the S&P 500 (SPX). MORT is down roughly -17% in the past month as stocks sold off, and the bond market suffered from the basis trade unwind:

In a surprising development in financial markets, long-term U.S. Treasury yields surged at least 20 basis points on Monday as traders dumped bonds, despite an ongoing selloff in equities. Bonds are generally seen as a safe-haven asset during such turbulence. The unusual selloff in bonds on Monday may be linked to the unwinding of the "basis trade," a complex strategy used by hedge funds that has raised concerns about market stability, Apollo Asset Management said. The basis trade involves betting on the price difference between Treasury securities and their corresponding Treasury futures contracts.

MORT was therefore caught in the 'perfect storm', where bonds and equities experienced a positive correlation, moving in the same direction. The result was a deep sell-off in the fund and its components:

The above is the technical graph for AGNC. We can see the name deeply oversold going through the lower Bollinger Band, and having an RSI of below 30. These technical situations cannot last, and they usually resolve themselves on the upside with an RSI normalization and a move up in price.

We can see the same technical pattern for MORT itself:

The price action has already started to correct itself on the upside, and we are targeting a 10.5 price per share, equivalent to the middle of the Bollinger Band in the graph above.

Technical analysis is purely a reflection of oversold market conditions, which come from fundamentals. In our case we had an equity risk-off event together with a move higher in rates, which is highly unusual. The bond market basis trade was a large contributor to this positive correlation.

Targets

As highlighted above, we do not intend to hold MORT, but buy it for a short term trade targeting $10.5/share. As highlighted above, we see MORT as a high beta cyclical ETF, and thus think the best is to trade the name around rather than take a buy and hold position. When doing shorter time-frame trades, it is important to set exact targets and exit the name when such targets are realized. Discipline is very important for such a strategy.

Conclusion

MORT is an exchange-traded fund. The name contains mReit and CRE Reit equity, and has been hit hard by the latest market turmoil. The name was dragged down by both the market risk-off event as well as bond yields moving higher. Technically in deep oversold territory, MORT is due for a short term bounce. The article outlines the employed strategy and metrics, with a 'Buy' rating in place targeting $10.5/share.

data.title: WCLD: Slightly Undervalued Cloud ETF And A Strategy For High Implied Volatility
data.description:
Article Thesis

I wrote about WisdomTree Cloud Computing Fund ETF (NASDAQ:WCLD) in October last year and assigned it a Hold status. It was trading around my Fair Value estimate, maybe very slightly below, based on an average fair value of the constituents.

However, there is an interesting growth ahead for the SaaS (Software as a Service) market, with a CAGR somewhere between ~13% and ~19% until 2030. A short-term tailwind was also expected to be the decline in interest rates. Small- and mid-cap stocks would benefit from that because they can access cheaper credit and their discount rate for future cash-flows would be lowered.

Based on the combination of these factors, I presented a combination of strategies through shares and options (more details below) which proved to be quite profitable, despite still relatively high interest rates. What about now, with this tariff chaos and increased chances of recession?

WCLD Portfolio and Peers

WCLD seeks to track the price and yield performance, before fees and expenses, of the BVP Nasdaq Emerging Cloud Index, an equally weighted index designed to measure the performance of emerging public companies focused on delivering cloud-based software to customers.

More than half of the portfolio is constituted by small-cap (12.4%) and mid-cap (46.4%) stocks. While the weight of large-cap companies did increase a bit from 39.05% to 41.25%, there are no mega-cap stocks.

Top 10 holdings have a portfolio weight of 17.3%, so there is no risk of portfolio concentration:

The closest peers that I found are Global X Cloud Computing ETF (CLOU) and First Trust Cloud Computing ETF (SKYY). These two are a little different because they do include very-large-cap and mega-cap (especially SKYY).

I am not a fan of SKYY because I generally prefer to have individual positions in a few of the large- and mega-cap companies, depending on valuation. I mentioned in October that their average valuation was higher than for small-and-mid-cap, and indeed SKYY underperformed in this period:

Initial Strategy

Based on the very slight undervaluation and interesting growth ahead for the SaaS industry, I presented three strategies in October:

Strategy #1: Directly buying the ETF at $31.95 (a very small position, since it was only insignificantly undervalued). As posted through a comment on February 4, I exited this position at $40.82, for about 28% ROI in about 4 months, a remarkable return for an ETF. However, there is a big IF: only if the position was closed around that date because now WCLD is even below the initial level.

Then, how did I know to close it close to the top? Of course, this is not always a guarantee, but the most important is valuation, it became about 20% overvalued, a bit too much for an ETF. The second factor was the threat of tariffs, storm clouds were already gathering in February. And finally, technical analysis pointed to a reversal pattern, a clear double top at around $41.25:

Strategy #2: A Covered Call strategy, with 100 WCLD shares and selling 1 WCLD 32 Call expiring December 20, 2024 (I liked that because Call options are more expensive when interest rates are high).

The position had a total cost of 3195 (100 shares) – 175 (Call premium) = $3020. With WCLD on December 20 above $32, I was assigned at $32, for a profit of 175 (Call premium) + 5 (price appreciation) = $180, for a ROI of about 6% in two and a half months.

Strategy #3: A partial Covered Call, with 80 WCLD shares and the same WCLD 32 Call Dec’24. This position has a bit higher return at exactly the strike price, due to a lower cost: 2556 (80 shares) – 175 (Call premium) = $2381. However, it comes with lower returns for higher prices and a break-even at $40.95:

Like presented in the article, with WCLD going up in November, I preferred to close that position when the price was at about the middle of that range 32 – 40.95 because in this way we can be sure to mark a profit, even if lower: 352 (price appreciation) – 300 (difference in Call premium) = $52, or about 2.2% in about one month and a half.

Valuation and Risks Today

For a relative valuation, let’s look at the most used multiple for growing SaaS companies: growth-adjusted EV/Sales:

With an average EV/Sales/Growth for the last three years between 0.45x and 0.5x (we need to exclude the COVID years) and a value today around 0.4x, that denotes an undervaluation of about 15%.

For an absolute Fair Value, according to Morningstar, the IT sector has an undervaluation of about 15%:

Small-cap stocks are more undervalued (about 21%) than large-cap peers (about 10%), but is that entirely justified? Regarding especially SaaS companies, they are not very much affected by tariffs (until now).

However, if we look in a second line at a possible recession triggered by these tariffs, then yes, small-cap stocks have a higher risk than large-cap ones. Another risk is brought by the possibility that interest rates will stay higher for longer if inflation will remain elevated. As mentioned in the introduction, this will affect more small-cap than large-cap stocks.

However, it is possible that these risks are already priced in (except for a severe recession). Since most WCLD constituents are mid-cap stocks, I’m going to assume about 15% undervaluation from the overall IT sector and backed by the relative valuation above.

A Strategy for April 2025

Due to this tariff chaos, IV (Implied Volatility) is today at extremely high levels, unseen from 2020 or from 2008:

When IV is that high, we can try to profit from selling options. While Call options are still a little more expensive than Put options due to high interest rates, I would try now to sell a Put option in a margin account because that brings leverage. For more information, in a recent article about Pfizer (PFE), I described in more detail some differences between a covered Call and a short Put.

The cost of a short Put in a margin account is a bit tricky. While there is a margin, that varies with the underlying price, usually between 10% to 20% of the strike price for an ATM (At-The-Money) option, and we need to give it more room to fall.

The question is: how much I see WCLD falling from today’s price in a scenario involving a serious recession (excluding catastrophic events which can terminate the stock market entirely)? Even if it is down considerably, and about 15% undervalued today, that is likely pricing in only a shallow recession. In a severe recession scenario, although not a base case scenario, I think this could go down another 30%, to about $21.4.

A 30 Put expiring September’25 was sold yesterday for $225. That means I need to set aside enough cash for a 30% drop from $30, or $900. So, the cost would be 900 – 225 = $675. But what happens if WCLD would plunge even more until September, under $21? Although this is extremely unlikely for an ETF (no, I don’t think that another Great Depression has chances, tariffs were only one of many factors back then), I always have a cash & equivalents account for rainy days, and it happens that this is way overfull for now, after closing many positions last year, or even a few of them last month.

However, I would target for now only a half-position (maximum), let's see how these risks will evolve.

Let’s see how the position would perform at expiration in different scenarios:

  • Maximum ROI is 225/675 = 33% (or about 80% annualized) with the price above $30
  • Break-even at $27.85
  • Under $27.85, I will either roll down the Put or switch to a more bullish strategy for an overall profit, based on a re-assessment
  • Under $21.4 (although extremely unlikely), more funds will be needed
  • On the upside, a stock position will beat it above $39.7 – unlikely until September, but possible if there will be a major relief from tariffs.

As usual, I will try to close the position early. If, for example, in one or two months, the Put would value less than 30% of its cost (price up or IV down), I will close it in advance, not worth it to keep it another three or four months for so little more.

Takeaway

WCLD has a portfolio of diversified SaaS stocks, with a focus on small- and mid-cap, avoiding mega-cap stocks, unlike its peers CLOU and SKYY. SaaS has double-digit long-term growth ahead, and small cap are well positioned for decreasing interest rates.

I presented in October three strategies. A direct ETF purchase followed by a sale in February yielded significant returns, by capitalizing on valuation insights, market conditions and technical analysis. A covered Call expiring Dec'24 strategy also performed well, although not as well as the underlying, while a partially covered Call (with 80 shares) brought the smallest profit, due to a lower exposure to the underlying.

WCLD seems today about 15% undervalued based on Morningstar Fair Value for the IT sector and for mid-cap stocks. The same 15% undervaluation derives from a growth-adjusted EV/Sales multiple compared to the last three years. With double-digit long-term growth expected, WCLD seems interesting again.

Current very high implied volatility offers an opportunity to sell Put options for higher premiums, and with leverage in a margin account, with a cautious approach given potential recession risks.

data.title: Liberty Energy: Q1-25 Beat And More Optimistic Than Expected Outlook, Stock Overdone To Downside
data.description:
Liberty Energy (NYSE:LBRT) reported better-than-expected Q1-25 results, beating consensus revenue and EBITDA by 2% and 7%, respectively.

The beat comes at a time when the entire North American service industry and completion-focused names in particular have fallen dramatically on trade war fears, the subsequent drop in oil and natural gas prices, and an ongoing slide in rig and frac spread counts. All frac spreads are not created equal (Liberty's fleets are predominately Tier IV DGB and efrac at the upper end of the market) but the following chart or similar versions of it have stoked investor fears.

While some slippage in activity and pricing is to be expected and investors have a right to feel cautious about the group's near-term prospects, the shares of LBRT are off 45% since the Q4-24 report in February. This is far more than current expectations, and the outlook included with the Q1-25 report was more positive than we expected. Here's a quick rundown of favorite quotes from the release to bear in mind before listening to the call, along with our thoughts on each.

Favorite Quotes Watch:

  • "We saw strong sequential improvement in utilization across our fleet"
    • Z4: This was telegraphed by Liberty and their peers on the Q4-24 round of frac player calls, and is also just seasonally normal.
  • "Our early year results demonstrate a positive rebound from the fourth quarter of 2024, a trend that has continued into the second quarter." "We started the year with positive momentum and are currently anticipating sequential growth in revenue and profitability in the second quarter from higher utilization."
    • Z4: The second comment is better than many likely expected, again, in light of the industry chart above. Note that consensus revenue for 2Q is $999 mm vs the $977 mm beat just reported. This should lead to very little in the way of near-quarter estimate drift. Comments on the call will be key in sorting out the second half of 2025, but so far, so good.
  • "Today, we have excess demand for Liberty services as our customers align themselves with top-tier providers in a clear industry ‘flight to quality,'"
    • Z4: The "excess demand" comment is also better than expected. Look for management to address the falling fleet counts as lower spec, largely Tier II losses.

Cautionary Quotes:

  • "While North American producers have not yet meaningfully changed development plans, we expect our customers to assess a range of scenarios in anticipation of commodity price pressure, and we are staying close to our partners in this dynamic market." "While the current tumult in commodity prices is not immediately driving changes in North American activity, we expect oil producers are evaluating a range of scenarios in anticipation of oil price pressure. Concurrently, gas producers could prove to be beneficiaries of potentially lower associated gas production in oily basins."
    • Z4: It's early, and the E&Ps have not reported yet, but our own informal polling suggests a small portion of the upstream will shrink capex, but many more remain on the fence, holding out for another quarter. No upstream player of size that we know will be looking to expand spending, but it's possible that we some in gassy basins suggest that later this year they could increase activity beyond their start of year plans. We expect to see some spreads leave the Permian for the gassy basins, resulting in more balanced pricing.

Macro Comment:

  • "Today’s frac activity simply supports maintenance of current oil production levels"
    • Z4: Given their past statements, this is a little surprising, as they previously expressed doubt that frac spread counts 10% higher than current levels may not be sufficient to maintain volumes.

Other Items:

Power Segment: Nothing really incremental.

Return of Capital:

  • On Tuesday they maintained their $0.08/quarter dividend (implied yield of 2.8%, second highest in this group after PTEN),
  • They bought back 1% of shares in the quarter, after buying 3.8% of shares last year.

Balance Sheet: Net debt to annualized Q1-25 EBITDA of 0.3, flat with prior quarter's metric.

Nutshell: Nice beat with margin improvement and a better-than-expected outlook. With the shares off 45% since the last quarter and consensus unlikely to move much and only down 8% and 9% for 2025 and 2026, respectively, since that call, we see the name as clearly overdone to the downside, leaving this solid balance sheet, solid dividend yield, and share repurchasing name trading below 3x for both this year and next year's EBITDA. We've included our Frac Comp table for a little more color as well.

data.title: Block: A 'Buy' On Valuation And Bitcoin Strategy
data.description:
Block, Inc (NYSE:XYZ) is a company that I've been quietly following for a while. During the COVID-era tech run in stocks, XYZ was one of the highest fliers due. The stock's inclusion in Cathie Wood's ARK Innovation ETF (ARKK) at a time when Wood's flagship fund grew to roughly $28 billion in AUM:

More recently, we've seen the price of Block, Inc come back down to earth following a retracement that has the stock more than 80% off it's all time high of $289 per share from August 2021. Interestingly enough, it seems as though some of the COVID-era high fliers have lived and died by Ark Invest flows. And as fate would have it, ARKK has been a net seller of XYZ for essentially 15 months straight:

ARKK holdings of XYZ are currently down to 2.3 million shares from just under 8 million in December 2023. The combined XYZ holdings through all of Cathie Wood's Ark Invest funds are 3.8 million shares, and the stock gets a weighting rank of 12. For a brief period in March 2024, that weighting rank was as high as 2. This begs the question, with Ark Invest seemingly losing interest in the stock, is it actually time to take a look at Block, Inc?

I think yes.

Block, Inc Business Segments

For followers of the stock, this will hopefully serve as a brief review. However, the stock is not one that I have covered before for Seeking Alpha. So for my own readers who may not be familiar with Block, Inc, the company primarily provides payment processing services and operates platforms for both B2C and P2P interactions. The B2C solution is Square and helps businesses accept credit card payments. Square's customer base is fairly diverse with 33% coming from the food beverage industry, 17% from retail, and a relatively even split among Professional Services, Personal Care, Health/Fitness, and Home & Repair.

Even within the categories themselves, Square's customer base is diverse as well, with no more than 5% of Gross Payment Volume - or GPV - coming from any one customer. That has been the case for 3 years running. The P2P product is Cash App. Cash App is the top finance app on Google Play and the second most downloaded app on iOS in the United States. Annual inflows through Cash App hit a new high in 2024 at $283 billion and have been growing at a 25% CAGR since 2020. However, it should be noted that monthly active user growth has been slowing down for several years:

At 57 million monthly active users in 2024, annual MAU growth over 2023 was just 1.8% and is admittedly slowing down considerably. 2024 MAU growth was down from 9.8% in 2023 and 15.9% in 2022. But again, inflow growth remains robust. Beyond the payment processing platforms Square and Cash App, Block also owns the music streaming service TIDAL and Bitcoin (BTC-USD) projects Bitkey and Proto, which aim to compete in the Bitcoin wallet and Bitcoin mining spaces.

Just Another Bitcoin-Adjacent Company?

Block, Inc founder and CEO Jack Dorsey has been a notable advocate for Bitcoin and that is reflected through both his own personal usage of the decentralized social network Nostr - which utilizes Bitcoin's Lightning Network for micropayments - as well as Block, Inc's corporate approach to Bitcoin. In addition to developing Bitkey and Proto, Cash App users can buy Bitcoin in increments as small as $1 and store the digital asset directly in the app through a custodial account. Cash App has also enabled Bitcoin deposits and withdrawals, as well as integration with Lightning Network. The company also owns 8,485 Bitcoin. Those coins have a market value of $710 million.

Unlike many of the other companies that have BTC stashes this significant, Bitcoin is not Block's sole focus and the BTC/Market Cap ratio for the company is just 2.1%. While this figure is inline with a company like Coinbase (COIN), XYZ really shouldn't be viewed as a pure Bitcoin-proxy the way Strategy (MSTR) might be. The stock has been correlated with the Nasdaq-100 more so than Bitcoin. The table below shows the monthly return correlation with the ProShares Bitcoin ETF (BITO) and the Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ) over the last three years:

36 Month Return Correlation Ticker XYZ QQQ BITO
Block, Inc XYZ 1 0.71 0.6
Invesco QQQ Trust QQQ 0.71 1 0.55
ProShares Bitcoin ETF BITO 0.6 0.55 1

Source: Portfolio Visualizer, 11/1/21-3/31/25

While BITO's correlation with the Q's is also evident at 55%, XYZ is closer to the Q's at 71% than it is to BITO at 60%. The point is, Block is really more of a company that uses Bitcoin rather than one that should be viewed as dependent on Bitcoin. As I see it, Block has put itself in a position to benefit from Bitcoin's potential success without over-leveraging the idea.

Revenue and Profitability

Breaking out Block's revenue by source, the company appears to be highly reliant on Bitcoin transactions, as the company's biggest revenue segment for each of the last three years has been Bitcoin:

Revenue (000s) 2024 2023 2022
Transaction-based $6,613,680 $6,315,301 $5,701,540
Subscription and Services $7,164,799 $5,944,842 $4,552,773
Hardware $143,369 $157,178 $164,418
Bitcoin $10,199,205 $9,498,302 $7,112,856
Total $24,121,053 $21,915,623 $17,531,587

Source: Block, Inc

However, I think this is a bit misleading because Block's gross margin on Bitcoin was just 2.8% in 2024 - up from 2.2% in both 2023 and 2022. Still, the overwhelming majority of Block's gross profit comes from Subscription and Services:

Gross Profit (000s) 2024 2023 2022
Transaction-based $2,732,667 $2,613,285 $2,337,512
Subscription and Services $6,028,986 $4,869,713 $3,691,028
Hardware -$93,072 -$110,472 -$122,577
Bitcoin $288,819 $205,189 $156,123
Total $8,889,036 $7,504,886 $5,991,892

Source: Block, Inc

At $6 billion in 2024 gross profit, Subscription and Services accounted for 68% of Block's gross profit in 2024. From an operating income standpoint, 2024 was a terrific year for Block, Inc:

With over $1 billion in operating income, 2024 was Block's best year by far. The same was true for earnings per share, which came in at $4.70 last year. Despite terrific growth in both operating and net income, XYZ remains near 52-week lows in share price. Part of the problem was Block missing the market's EPS expectations in Q4 and forward guidance that was not well received.

Valuation

Despite slowing growth in Cash App MAUs and a disappointing end to 2024, I think XYZ is worth 'buy' at this valuation. For a broader stock market that has become overvalued based on metrics like Market Cap to GDP and Stocks to Bonds ratios, Block, Inc might actually be somewhat attractive at current levels:

Against an S&P 500 PE ratio of 26 times earnings, XYZ looks good. Especially if the company can beat expectations in 2025. XYZ is currently trading at just 12 times trailing earnings and 23 times forward earnings. More broadly, Seeking Alpha gives XYZ a 'C-' overall valuation grade, with mixed grades that vary between 'A+' and 'F.' At a price to forward cash flow of 10.5, my view is XYZ is closer to 'cheap' than 'expensive.'

Risks

One of the things that does concern me about Block is the company has added 'buy now pay later' loans through Cash App. Such services have grown in recent years and add credit risk from a consumer cohort that could be exhibiting signs of being 'tapped out' financially. The bigger concern, though, is perhaps the potential of a recession in 2025. The market seems very uncertain about what impact tariffs will have on the global economy, and there are certainly indications that the major indexes could still find new lows this year. XYZ will not be immune from a further decline in the broader equity market.

Closing Thoughts

Like I said in my Coinbase piece earlier this month, panic in the streets can present opportunity. And like COIN, I think a long term speculative position in XYZ looks a lot better after what has been a steep selloff. Specifically for Block, I believe the company has been de-risked a bit due to a variety of factors. First, the valuations are far more realistic than they were back when the company was benefitting from ARK Invest AUM growth. Second, Block is making an effort to better integrate across its platforms. Third, my view is the company is exhibiting a very sensible Bitcoin strategy where it can benefit from BTC adoption but not necessarily be at the mercy of it. As a treasury instrument, the company has been far more conservative in growing its BTC stack than some of the other companies with significant holdings in the asset. I'm initiating coverage with a 'buy' rating.

data.title: Applied Digital: 3 Reasons We Think Now Is The Time To Buy
data.description:
We've had our eyes on Applied Digital (NASDAQ:APLD) for quite a while.

Initially set up as a cryptocurrency mining solutions outfit, the firm has since pivoted to becoming a 'digital infrastructure' provider, offering Cloud and AI data center space to HPC (High Performance Compute) customers.

Operating in three distinct segments, the company still retains some crypto exposure, but much of the firm's focus now rests squarely on Ellendale, North Dakota. Why?

Because APLD is building a massive data center there, where it plans to offer custom-built, AI-first data center infrastructure to hyperscale customers.

With solid revenue growth, improving margins, and a fantastic future opportunity set, why did the stock drop 10% overnight on earnings?

In short, the company has been having trouble wooing hyperscale customers to actually sign a lease and begin using the data center. With hundreds of millions of dollars poured into the campus and lease negotiations dragging on, investors are starting to get antsy about seeing an ROI. Will the new data center campus ever produce real revenues?

For our part, we see APLD as being in a much better position than it first appears on paper.

While the leasing negotiations for Ellendale are taking a while, the company's sound financial footing and broader technological trends position the stock well for solid returns to shareholders.

Today, we'll outline APLD's financials, explore the recent earnings, and explain three reasons why we think APLD's stock is a 'Buy' in the current market environment.

Sound good? Let's dive in.

Reason 1: APLD's Financials

Before talking about Ellendale and the new data center opportunity, it's worth first mentioning a bit about APLD's history and business profile.

Essentially, the company operates as a multi-pronged digital infrastructure company, offering three key products across three core segments: Crypto Hosting, Cloud Hosting, and HPC Hosting:

We are a U.S. designer, developer, and operator of next-generation digital infrastructure across North America. We provide digital infrastructure solutions and Cloud services to the rapidly growing industries of High-Performance Computing ("HPC") and Artificial Intelligence ("AI"). We operate in three distinct business segments, Blockchain data center hosting (the "Data Center Hosting Business"), cloud services primarily through a wholly-owned subsidiary (the "Cloud Services Business") and HPC data center hosting (the "HPC Hosting Business").

For reference, APLD doesn't actually offer compute to clients, it builds out data centers for use by clients. This means that APLD doesn't own the chips in its data centers, it owns the real estate, buildings, and physical infrastructure. It also integrates the power, cooling, and other needs of the client that will end up actually offering the compute, one level up the value chain.

This differs materially from a company like CoreWeave (CRWV), which owns and operates the real estate, data centers, power supply, cloud platform, chips, and more, in a single vertical stack.

APLD is much closer to a REIT, in that it builds, supplies and leases specialized real estate to those who want to use it, somewhat akin to firms like Equinix (EQIX) and Digital Realty Trust (DLR).

Financially, the company has been doing relatively well since inception. On the one hand, there have been some challenges. Strong dilution has put a damper on the share price, and materially negative-looking operating results have driven some investors away:

However, while the company appears highly dilutive and unprofitable, in reality, the firm's financials are in a far better place than feared.

Here's a view of the company's income statement and margins:

As you can see, cost of revenues is sky-high, which (in theory) doesn't leave much room for interest payment costs, SG&A, or other operating expenses.

That said, if you dig into the weeds, you'll find that a massive portion of APLD's cost base is depreciation, which is a non-cash expense that the firm is adding into upper-level income statement sections:

Occasionally, earnings numbers don't reflect what's happening on the ground. This is one of those cases. If you look at APLD on a cash basis, we think that company is in good shape.

If you add up all of APLD's D&A costs from Q3, it comes out to roughly $18 million, which is substantial. Once you take out all of the company's other 'noncash' stuff, you end up with a materially positive adjusted EBITDA number:

Of course, when looking at a company like this, there's a danger of relying on an adjusted EBITDA number when recurring expenses may actually be recurring or costly in other ways to the business. We're not fans of doing it ourselves.

However, if you look a quarter back, you'll find the firm isn't really abusing this figure, and similarly high D&A figures continue to scrape off considerable operating momentum:

Thus, given that the Adjusted EBITDA numbers are positive, we believe that the company is actually in a fine position with respect to liquidity and solvency.

Simultaneously, growing revenues support a structurally positive margin profile:

Taken together, we're confident in APLD's overall financial health and profitability going forward. This stability is what is allowing the company to go after big fish.

At present, the company's Cloud and Crypto hosting segments are holding up tent, but future growth is clearly focused on a single point: Ellendale.

Reason 2: Ellendale

If we were to re-write APLD's recent earnings performance to reflect cash realities, then the company's growing revenues and subsequent positive cash EBITDA of $21 million and $10 million are more than enough to keep everything running.

This is powering the financing bonanza that APLD is using to fund Ellendale:

As you can see, APLD is dropping considerable Capex on this endeavor, mostly funded by debt.

This leverage is why investors are starting to get antsy about the lack of a newly signed lease.

On the recent earnings call, with respect to this pivotal deal, APLD management had the following to say:

In our HPC Hosting segment, we have achieved significant milestones in advancing our strategic objectives. [We inked a] $375 million financing arrangement with Sumitomo Mitsui Bank Corporation, one of Japan's top three banking groups and a global leader in data center financing...

We believe that Ellendale campus represents a highly strategic industry asset with significant expansion opportunities beyond the initial 400 megawatts of critical IT load. Importantly, our construction remains on schedule for our first building, and we expect it to be ready for service and ready to begin generating revenue in the calendar fourth quarter of 2025. Nearly all the equipment for this building is landed, giving us not only confidence in the schedule, but it also means tariffs will not materially impact our build cost.

For our part, it appears as though the market wanted a 'firmer' answer to come this quarter with respect to securing a lease, but frankly, deals this large take time to finalize. Given APLD's status as a first-time provider to hyperscale customers, we'd expect a thorough vetting would take time.

Going forward, we expect that a finalized deal would have a massive impact on APLD. Sure, the revenue growth is a material piece of the puzzle, but beyond that, if the company can 'crack' the market, then they'll be able to more easily garner future deals. The effect is multiplicative.

Analysts are expecting bumpy earnings growth going forward, but we hold a more positive outlook:

If APLD can get the deal signed, then we're looking at revenue growth in the upper twenties or lower thirties range YoY, which could pair with huge EPS growth as the company's results swing more firmly into the GAAP black. It's too speculative to give a precise estimate for exactly how or to what degree this might happen, but the company's financial statements are set up for a boost in revenue to wash everything down with cash.

This could drive serious appreciation for investors.

Reason 3: The Valuation

Finally, the company's valuation appears attractive, especially following the recent, earnings-induced selloff.

We think APLD has room to drive appreciation through organic results alone that would result from the completion of a deal. We've talked about this.

However, the valuation also appears attractive, which opens up a second potential route for gains: multiple expansion.

On the top line, APLD sports a TTM sales multiple of roughly 5.3x, which appears relatively low versus the nominal growth opportunity:

Similarly, on the bottom line, if you extrapolate, APLD could produce Adjusted EBITDA of $50 million or more this fiscal year.

Next year, this could grow considerably, as management expect Ellendale to begin producing revenues in Q4. This could result in EBITDA of $80 million or more in FY '26. This puts the current year EBITDA multiple at roughly 24x, and the FWD multiple at roughly 15x.

Given the longer-term growth potential in providing digital real estate, these appear to be attractive entry points into the broader digital infrastructure trend and story here.

APLD isn't rated well by more quantitative screens, and Seeking Alpha's quant rating system gives the stock a 'D+':

However, in our view, this is largely skewed by the company's ugly GAAP figures. When you plug in operating metrics that are more reflective of what is happening on the ground, we'd argue APLD is a solid value at present.

Risks

Of course, there are risks associated with a 'Buy' in the stock.

The core risk is quite well outlined already - the potential for APLD to have its expensive Capex and data center sit dormant for any real length of time.

If you look at APLD's infrastructure spend, the depth of the company's investment has been considerable:

If this expensive outlay doesn't begin generating ROI, or somehow proves that it doesn't have product-market fit, then APLD could be in serious trouble, given the outstanding debt balance.

Additionally, APLD has some headline risk, in that the stock is one of a handful that NVIDIA (NVDA) has invested in. Since building out data center infrastructure is in their best interest as well, the investment and partnership is a positive narrative for the company. If NVDA drops this stake at any point, we expect that the market would take it as a bad sign. If they drop the stake before APLD is able to sign an Ellendale lease, then it could be a sign that NVDA doesn't see the company being able to lure true hyperscale clientele to the campus, which would be extremely negative.

We don't see this happening, at least in the short term, but there is some serious headline risk here.

Finally, we believe that APLD's valuation is attractive, but it could get cheaper and cheaper if the company continues to fail to find a tenant for Ellendale. This could provide headwinds to appreciation going forward.

Summary

Overall, though, we think that APLD is well positioned to grow top and bottom line performance on the back of in-demand digital infrastructure. Recent Capex growth is a sign that the company is meeting targets and deadlines, and the recent drop appears to be a good price at which one can pick up shares in a solid long-term growth story.

Sure, there are risks, but in our view, the potential rewards outweigh them.

Thus, our 'Buy' rating.

Cheers!

data.title: Lock In The 9.8% Yield Of AGNC's Preferred Stock Before It Decreases
data.description:
The preferred stock of AGNC Investment (NASDAQ:AGNCO) has outperformed the broad market this year, as it has offered a total return of -1% whereas the S&P 500 has declined 8%. After two years of excessive returns, the S&P 500 has entered a tumultuous period characterized by high uncertainty, as the new U.S. government is in the process of raising tariffs to levels not seen since the 1930s. Given also the recent surge in volatility in stock prices, it is only natural that most conservative investors hesitate to invest in common stocks.

On the other hand, waiting on the sidelines has proved disastrous in most years, and it is highly risky, as one runs the risk of missing the train of the recovery. Decades of experience have shown that the stock market recovers much earlier than the first signs of an economic recovery appear on the horizon. Waiting on the sidelines is even worse in the current economic environment, as inflation erodes the real value of cash at a fast pace.

In this investing landscape, the aforementioned preferred stock of AGNC Investment is an attractive candidate for conservative investors or income-oriented investors. The preferred stock is currently offering a 9.8% dividend, with a margin of safety. Even better, if inflation and interest rates rise due to the implementation of tariffs, the dividend yield of the preferred stock will rise thanks to its floating formula. Overall, investors should consider buying the preferred stock of AGNC Investment before its yield drops.

Overview of the preferred stock

AGNC Investment (AGNC) has total common equity capital of $8.1 billion and total preferred equity of $1.6 billion. It has five different preferred shares, with AGNCO comprising $403 million of preferred capital, or 4% of the total capital of the company.

Source: Investor Presentation

AGNCO has a floating yield equal to 0.26161%+4.993%+3-month SOFR rate. The latter is currently standing at 4.39%. Given also that AGNCO is trading at a 2% discount to its par value of $25, the preferred stock is offering a 9.8% yield.

In reference to the other preferred stocks of AGNC, AGNCL is offering a fixed yield of 8.4% while AGNCN, AGNCM and AGNCP are offering floating yields of 9.8%, 9.4% and 9.6%, respectively. Going forward, the article will focus on AGNCO, but the analysis is valid for AGNCN as well.

When a stock offers an abnormally high yield, it usually signals that its dividend is likely to be cut upon the first headwind. However, this is not the case for AGNCO. AGNC Investment does not have the right to cut its preferred dividend unless it first eliminates its common dividend. Fortunately for the preferred shareholders, AGNC Investment pays $1.24 billion per year in total for dividends but only $39 million for the dividends of AGNCO. Therefore, the dividends of AGNCO are only 3% of the total dividends paid by the company. If AGNC Investment has to preserve funds at some point in the future, it will be able to save ample funds by reducing its common dividend, without the need to suspend its preferred dividends.

It is also important to note that AGNC has never eliminated its common dividend since its foundation in 2008. Even during the worst mortgage crisis of the past 90 years, in 2008-2009, the company did not cut its common dividend.

Moreover, while AGNC Investment is facing challenges due to high interest rates, it exhibits decent business momentum. In the fourth quarter, its average yield on its assets improved sequentially from 4.73% to 4.80%, but its average cost of funds increased from 2.52% to 2.89%. As a result, operating earnings per share decreased 14%, from $0.43 to $0.37, and missed the analysts' estimates by $0.05.

Due to the headwind from high interest rates, analysts expect AGNC to incur a 15% decrease in its earnings per share this year, from $1.88 to $1.60. The expected decrease is in line with the performance of the company in the most recent quarter. If AGNC meets the analysts' estimates this year, the payout ratio of the common dividend will surge from 77% to 90%. It is thus evident that the common dividend is not safe. However, it is far from being eliminated and hence the preferred dividend of AGNC has a margin of safety.

This is the key advantage of a preferred stock vs. a common stock. The common stock of AGNC has been hurt by the decrease in earnings but preferred shareholders have nothing to worry about as long as the company is far from going bankrupt. AGNC is expected by analysts to remain highly profitable in 2025-2027 and hence the preferred dividend appears safe.

Business risks

The economic outlook has significantly deteriorated lately. The U.S. government is trying to boost domestic production by engaging in a trade war with most of the trading partners of the country. The tariffs it has announced are the highest since the 1930s. These tariffs are likely to result in higher inflation, partly due to higher production costs, as some raw materials are imported. In addition, consumers are likely to reduce their spending. As consumption comprises 70% of GDP, lower consumer spending may cause a recession. Goldman Sachs recently raised the odds of a recession within the next 12 months from 20% to 45%.

On the bright side, the U.S. government has softened its stance in the last few days. Its aggressive policy caused a plunge in the prices of U.S. bonds, and thus bond yields skyrocketed. If the government did not soften its stance, the U.S. would have to incur a sharp increase in interest expense until the markets calmed down. Fortunately, the government has realized its vulnerability and is now trying to negotiate with its trading partners without pushing things to the extremes. Therefore, a severe recession appears unlikely in my view.

Even if a recession shows up this or next year, it is not likely to lead AGNC Investment to eliminate its common dividend and suspend its preferred dividends. The company did not eliminate its common dividend even during the Great Recession and the coronavirus crisis.

Another risk is the unfavorable scenario of a resurgence of inflation due to the implementation of tariffs. In such a case, the Fed may have to turn around and raise interest rates instead of reducing them. Consequently, the borrowing costs of AGNC Investment will increase and its net interest margin may shrink.

While such a development will be negative for AGNC Investment, the company has endured a similar downturn without any problem. In 2022, when inflation surged to a 40-year high and the Fed raised interest rates at an almost unprecedented pace, AGNC Investment kept its common dividend intact. Therefore, even if inflation and interest rates increase materially from current levels, they are not likely to force AGNC Investment to suspend its common dividend. It is also worth noting that the yield of AGNCO will rise in the event of higher interest rates thanks to the floating yield of the preferred stock.

Overall, the economic environment has become much more uncertain than it was in 2023-2024. It is extremely hard to predict whether the risk to the economy is to the upside (inflation) or downside (recession) or a combination of the two (stagflation). The good news is that the government finally appears to be a hostage of the bond market, which is ready to punish U.S. treasuries with a fierce sell-off whenever the government toughens its stance. Therefore, it seems that we will probably avoid an extreme economic scenario, one that would put the preferred dividend of AGNC under pressure.

Final thoughts

It is well known that the S&P 500 has offered an average annual total return of approximately 10% over the long run. It is reasonable to expect the benchmark to continue offering a similar return over the long term. However, the stock market has gone through a few lost decades, such as those of 1970-1980 and 2000-2010. The excessive returns over the last 16 years and the ongoing financial turmoil cause many investors to hesitate to invest in common stocks right now. A great alternative for these investors is the preferred stock of AGNC, which is offering a 9.8% dividend with a margin of safety.

data.title: Cadiz: Are The Preferreds Back In Play?
data.description:
Cadiz (NASDAQ:CDZI) rallied hard to begin 2025, but common shares are now down 50% year-to-date to reflect a broader market zeitgeist defined by capital flight from risky tickers and into safer haven assets as a recession comes into the horizon. Los Angeles-based CDZI owns 45,000 acres of land in the Mojave Desert that overlies a large aquifer. The aquifer is located at the base of a 2000 square mile watershed with around 30 million to 50 million acre-feet of water in storage and represents the only new water supply in the Colorado River Basin. The project faces intense environmental opposition to pump and market water from the aquifer due to concerns about the damage it would pose to the unique fauna and flora of the Mojave.

CDZI at its current market cap of $212 million reflects some lingering investor enthusiasm around the material economic potential of monetizing its groundwater assets. CDZI is pumping capital into its plan, buying 180 miles of pipeline assets from the terminated Keystone XL Pipeline Project in November. The company has an ambitious plan to build a pipeline network connecting its groundwater bank in the Mojave to the water networks in the Southwestern US. While CDZI floated a letter of intent with a non-profit investment fund for an investment of up to $150 million for the build-out, ownership, and operation of its Mojave Groundwater Bank project, there have been no major updates since November.

Liquidity, Equity Dilution, And The Preferreds

Critically, the project still needs approval for commercial operations, a feat that has escaped management for years. While the new federal government could be more positive on providing the pipeline right-of-way, CDZI still faces headwinds from the state of California with Senate Bill 307. The bill, signed into law in August 2019, was essentially specifically created to target CDZI. It will prevent the company from commercial-scale pipeline exports from the Mojave aquifer without state review to verify that no adverse impact would occur to the desert ecosystem. CDZI is building out four business segments with its existing Cadiz Ranch and ATEC Water Systems segments helping to generate fiscal 2024 fourth quarter revenue of $4.8 million, up materially from $700,000 a year ago with net income in the period negative at $8.6 million.

CDZI ended the fourth quarter with cash and cash equivalents of $17.3 million, up 284% from its year-ago comp on the back of the issuance of new shares in 2024. This dilution has come to fully define the company's relationship with the public market. CDZI ended the fourth quarter with its diluted weighted shares outstanding at 72.6 million, up 8.8% from its year-ago comp and by 4.6 million sequentially. The shares have ballooned at a compound annual growth rate of 109% over the last 5 years, and are set to move higher with CDZI recently announcing a $20 million registered direct offering. This was for 5.715 million of its common shares at $3.50 per share.

The steep rates of dilution reflect a company with a trailing 12-month negative free cash flow profile of $22.47 million despite revenue of just $9.61 million in the same period. CDZI holds an inherently valuable asset in a state that suffers from serious water scarcity. However, the limited pathway for commercial start prevents an investment in CDZI from being prudent. Shareholders can expect another round of dilution in roughly a year, with the funds raised enough to fund roughly another year of operations at the company's current free cash flow burn rate. CDZI held total debt of $56.7 million as of the end of the fourth quarter.

Against the continued marked dilution of the common shareholders, the company's 8.875% Series A Cumulative Preferred (NASDAQ:CDZIP) offers a way to play the fortunes of the Mojave Groundwater Bank. These pay out a $2.21875 per share annual coupon for what's currently a 12.8% yield on cost. They are swapping hands for $17.29 per share, around a 30% discount to their $25 per share liquidation value, around 70 cents on the dollar. Both the common shares and preferreds have dipped markedly since I last covered the stock, reflecting a market captured by uncertainty around the ramping tariff regime of the 47th president and the ongoing trade war with China. Critically, if you think CDZI will eventually be able to get commercial operations up then the commons offer the better asymmetric bet. The continuation of the status quo of continually issuing new shares to underwrite free cash burn would place the preferreds with their double-digit yield as the better play. Hence, the common shares are rated a sell with the preferreds a hold.

data.title: Stitch Fix: The Worst Is Probably Over (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
It’s no understatement to say that the impending specter of tariffs, once renegotiated after the reprieve period, will disrupt global supply chains and reorder the world economy. And yet even with that said, though the stock market has bounced back from early April lows, many small and mid-cap stocks are stuck at penalty-box levels they likely don’t deserve.

Stitch Fix (NASDAQ:SFIX) is one of these companies. The stylist-driven e-commerce platform has seen a painful shedding of its customer base, but its declines are slowing alongside an often-overlooked return to profitability growth. The stock is down ~30% since the start of January, cutting deeply into a rebound rally that began in 2024.

I last wrote a sell opinion on Stitch Fix in January, when the stock was trading much higher at $5 per share. Now hovering just barely above $3, I believe a lot of the risks for Stitch Fix are already priced in. To that end, I’m upgrading my viewpoint on Stitch Fix to a neutral rating. Don’t rush in to buy here, but the stock has finally fallen to levels that are worth at least monitoring and putting on your watch list.

To me now, the stock is a more balanced mix of positives and negatives. To be sure, one major risk got added to the downside column for Stitch Fix: the risk of tariffs. Stitch Fix manages a complex supply chain, producing clothes under its own store brand while sourcing supply from various other brands that have different levels of exposure to recourse tariff rates that are presumably now being re-negotiated.

The risk of tariff hits, and the tough choice of balancing out absorbing the price increases and hurting margin or denting demand by passing on the increases to consumers, is likely to cause Stitch Fix to drop its near-term outlook. This risk is also stacked on top of an already-weak business, one that has been consistently shedding active customers for years.

And yet underneath these risks, there are glimmers of potential hope. Stitch Fix is gaining traction and seeing revenue growth in the men’s category - which, incidentally, is a bright spot in other more popular brands that are ceding share in women’s, like Lululemon (Stitch Fix, by the way, stocks a major rising Lululemon activewear competitor, Vuori).

And, in cutting down on performance marketing spend but maintaining the balance of stylist-client interactions that makes Stitch Fix still rather unique in a crowded e-commerce arena, the company is also making meaningful gains in profitability.

It’s also cheap, even though we expect hits to expectations and guidance. At current share prices just north of $3, Stitch Fix trades at a market cap of $398.0 million. Netting off the $229.8 million of cash on Stitch Fix’s balance sheet against zero current debt gives us an enterprise value of $168.2 million. This fact can’t be understated: most of Stitch Fix’s current market value sits in its cash balances.

For the remainder of the year FY25, which ends in August, Stitch Fix has guided to -6.9% to -5.8% revenue decline (adjusted for a tough comp against a 53-week fiscal year in FY24), and a 3.3-3.8% adjusted EBITDA margin, the midpoint of which implies $43.5 million in adjusted EBITDA.

This means Stitch Fix trades at just 3.9x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA - an almost unheard of valuation multiple, even with the recent stock market correction.

Of course, as just mentioned, Stitch Fix faces the prospect of tariffs that stack on top of the company’s slew of existing risks: but at the same time, its recent trend of profit growth (at a very cheap multiple of EBITDA) is promising. To me, that low valuation makes me believe that Stitch Fix is at or is close to finding the near-term floor for its share price.

Q2 download

Let’s now go through Stitch Fix’s latest fiscal Q2 (February quarter) results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

Stitch Fix's revenue declined -5.5% y/y to $312.1 million, though this achieved a significant beat over Wall Street's expectations of just $298.0 million (-9.8% y/y) by more than four point margin. It's worth noting as well that Stitch Fix's decline improved meaningfully from a -13% decline in Q1.

One of the core issues for Stitch Fix is that the company continues to lose active clients, which it defines as somebody who has made a purchase within the trailing twelve months. The company counted 2.37 million active customers at the end of Q2, which shed 63k customers from Q1 and is own -15% y/y.

There is a silver lining, however. Revenue per active client has increased 4% y/y to $537, and has seen consistent improvement. The company notes that order frequency among its most loyal customer base is improving, which at least helps to justify the company's expensive investments into its signature feature: its stylists who help clients choose what products to buy.

In addition to this, the company is noting several segments that have returned to growth: its men's segment, as well as its "Freestyle" segment - which is traditional e-commerce from which clients can pick and choose their own clothes, without ordering one of the company's signature "Fix" boxes.

Stitch Fix's gross margins are also on an upward trend, up 90bps y/y to 44.5%. This contrasts well against many of Stitch Fix's peers in the retail industry, which have delved deeper into promotions during the holiday quarter to attract an increasingly promo-driven customer.

Of course, all of this is before tariffs kick in. Stitch Fix has a mix of brand partners and suppliers, but it's likely that a good chunk of its product is sourced from China, Vietnam, or other countries that Trump has labeled as "bad actors" that will get hit by reciprocal tariffs once the reprieve window ends. Still, we like the fact that Stitch Fix is improving its structural profitability - adjusted EBITDA roughly tripled in the quarter on a y/y basis to $15.9 million - which gives Stitch Fix a little more cushion to absorb a demand slowdown or margin hits once tariffs take place.

Key takeaways

I'm not necessarily advocating an all-out rush to buy Stitch Fix on this recent dip. I think the markets still need time to digest the latest tariff negotiations and see the impacts roll through as companies update their guidance to reflect the macro risks, and Stitch Fix is no exception. At the same time, investors should also recognize that Stitch Fix's huge fall from YTD peaks has left the stock quite cheap, while at the same time the company has several pockets of growth like men's alongside healthy profit levels. Keep a close eye on this name.

data.title: Booking Stock: This Travel Titan Quietly Prints Cash
data.description:
The travel industry gives me mixed feelings. On the one hand, it's an industry that has a lot to grow in the coming years (especially online bookings) with very favorable trends such as the expansion of people's incomes and the shift in consumer preference for experiences.

At the same time, it is an industry that is very sensitive to the macroeconomic scenario, since economic pressures make travel less frequent and less costly alternatives are chosen, affecting the companies' revenue, so it can fit into a “superfluous” industry that generally has a lot of competition and hardly any players with very relevant differentials.

It turns out that the case of Booking Holdings (NASDAQ:BKNG) (NEOE:BKNG:CA) manages to mitigate these risks and extract the best from this industry through wide moats.

Booking Holdings: Wide Moats + Great Trends

Booking Holdings is by far the largest OTA in the world, in terms of market cap, revenue, and market share. For instance, Booking's market share in the European industry in 2024 was 69.3%. And that's also because the company not only owns the Booking brand, but also Agoda, Kayak, and others, which already shows the consolidation of the company and the industry in general.

The competitive advantages revolve around the size the company has acquired and can be summed up by the term network effect. But to break it down, it's the company's power to have both millions of users and a gigantic base of hotel rooms and other suppliers, creating a robust and practically irreplicable ecosystem. From this, also derives other competitive advantages such as the ability to build customer loyalty with loyalty programs, such as Genius, and the company's ability to offer the best hotel prices due to its solid partnerships with hotels that allow for good deals, and due to its scale and efficiency.

The business model is also very positive. Net income is already very attractive, with a margin of over 20%, but free cash flow is even higher due to the dynamics of working capital (in some cases it receives the money from the customer and then passes it on to the hotel), as well as depreciation and stock-based compensation.

In other words, this business model allows for absurd cash generation, and consequently returns capital to the shareholder through buybacks and dividends, as the chart below shows. Note how the share count keeps falling quarter by quarter, even with a substantial SBC.

As the main player in this industry, Booking has a lot to capture from overall market size growth, i.e., it's not even necessary to consider further market share gains for the prospects to already be positive. For example, Sky Quest estimates a CAGR of 12.3% between 2024 and 2032 for the Online Travel market size, explained by global trends such as higher disposable income, expanding social media influence and changing consumer habits, for example, with only the continuing trend of migration from physical booking to online booking, Booking Holdings already has growth to capture in the coming years.

Key Risks For Booking Holdings

Even at this size, the risks of Booking's thesis should not be ignored.

In addition to the risks of industry slowdown due to macroeconomic factors and other regulatory risks, the key risks come from competition.

Well, in the section above I mentioned that its size guarantees bargaining power and competitive advantages, and this mitigates competition. However, this doesn't block the company from technological disruption, as Booking itself has done to the industry.

One possible threat is Google (GOOGL) through Google Travel. Even a loyal Booking customer can easily google the date of their accommodation and flight, seeing dozens of other players, and this also affects the paid traffic that Booking.com uses.

AI is also something that is constantly mentioned, both on the positive and negative side. In the earnings call, this topic was addressed as something interesting, as the company continues to invest in these initiatives:

“[...]We are highly focused on the many opportunities with AI and we'll continue the sophisticated work already happening across our company to integrate generative AI into our offerings, which includes Booking.com's AI trip planner and Priceline's AI powered travel assistant called Penny.[...]”

But there is also a downside. Personal assistants like Amazon's Alexa (AMZN) or Google's Gemini could eventually make booking more practical. At first, it seems quite intangible, since people like to see reviews, photos of rooms and not just ask the AI assistant to book the trip for day x at price y, but still, these assistants may become relevant in the (possibly) distant future, suggesting the best rooms according to some algorithm, showing photos, optimizing routes and so on.

As intangible as all this may seem, we can't completely disregard the risks, since Booking, as good a company as it is, is ultimately an intermediary (albeit a necessary intermediary).

Booking Stock Offers a Reasonable Margin Of Safety

Even with the craziness of the stock market lately, BKNG stock hasn't seen many dips. Even so, the company manages to present an attractive enough margin of safety.

To try to find the potential return for the shareholder, I've drawn up a scenario that I consider feasible (and even conservative).

As mentioned throughout the article, the industry still has a lot to grow over the next few years, possibly reaching a CAGR of over 10%. Booking also has the potential to maintain growth close to this, not only by keeping pace with market growth and consolidating further but also by coming up with new forms of revenue through the maturing of cross-sell and up-sell (insurance, rent-a-car, etc.). As the industry is cyclical, and part of this must be considered optionality, I chose to estimate a more conservative growth.

For margins, there is a good opportunity over the next few years. The company has already achieved a net income margin of over 30% but is currently close to 25%. For the next few years, a better revenue mix (with a model where the company charges and then pays the hotel) could increase the take rate, and greater efficiency in marketing could increase the margin. Not to mention the more organic part, such as continued operational leverage through scale and gains from automation and AI optimizations. I have therefore estimated a gradually advancing margin, reaching 35% as a proxy for “full potential”.

These assumptions lead us to an earnings yield above 6% in 2028, and above 8.5% in 2030. Although we can see that part of the growth is already priced in, this earnings yield for a company that remunerates shareholders well, has great prospects and wide moats, is already minimally interesting, but is made even more so by the fact that the free cash flow is even higher than this, i.e., the potential for shareholder remuneration would be even greater in this scenario.

The Bottom Line

Even in an industry that is complex to operate in and well impacted by macroeconomic factors, Booking has established itself as a high-quality company with good fundamentals, competitive advantages, and very strong execution in recent years.

Although part of the optimism is already built into the price, the prospects for the company are reliable and have room to continue to surprise positively if Booking's execution remains strong. For this reason and the expectations of a high shareholder yield in the coming years, BKNG stock deserves a buy rating.
